title: IR-2 Child Visa Journey
description: Step-by-step roadmap for U.S. citizens petitioning for an unmarried child under 21 to immigrate via consular processing.

scenarioNotes.bio: You are petitioning your biological child. Steps will include birth certificate documentation.
scenarioNotes.step: You are petitioning a stepchild. A qualifying marriage between the U.S. citizen petitioner and the child's biological parent must have occurred before the child turned 18. See INA § 101(b)(1)(B).
scenarioNotes.adopted: You are petitioning an adopted child. The adoption must generally have been finalized before the child turned 16, and the child must have been in the petitioner’s legal custody for at least 2 years. See INA § 101(b)(1)(E). Consult an immigration attorney for complex adoption cases.

stages.name: Stage I: USCIS Petition (I-130)
stages.description: U.S. citizen petitioner files Form I-130 to establish qualifying relationship with child

steps.name: Confirm Petitioner Eligibility
steps.description: Only U.S. citizens may petition for an immediate relative child (IR-2). Lawful permanent residents cannot petition under the IR-2 immediate relative category — they must use a different family preference category (F-2A). The petitioner must be a U.S. citizen.
actions: Confirm petitioner is a U.S. citizen (by birth, naturalization, or acquisition)
Locate proof of citizenship: U.S. passport, U.S. birth certificate, or USCIS naturalization/citizenship certificate
Review the immediate relative child definition at uscis.gov/green-card/green-card-eligibility/green-card-immediate-relative-us-citizen
Confirm the child is unmarried and under 21 years of age at the time of filing
steps.output: Petitioner confirmed as U.S. citizen; child confirmed as unmarried and under 21

steps.name: Understand the IR-2 Visa Category
steps.description: IR-2 is the immediate relative immigrant visa category for an unmarried child under 21 of a U.S. citizen. As an immediate relative, there is no visa number waiting line — the visa is available immediately upon I-130 approval and NVC completion.
steps.info: Note on CR-2: The CR-2 category refers to a child who immigrates as a derivative of a conditional resident (CR-1) parent. If you are petitioning your child directly as a U.S. citizen, the applicable category is IR-2, not CR-2.
actions: Read the visa category definitions at travel.state.gov (Immigrant Visa Types)
Confirm the child qualifies as an immediate relative (unmarried, under 21, child of U.S. citizen)
Note that the child must remain unmarried and under 21 at the time of visa issuance and U.S. admission
If the child may turn 21 before the process completes, research the Child Status Protection Act (CSPA) at uscis.gov
steps.output: IR-2 visa category confirmed as applicable; CSPA age-out risk assessed

steps.name: Confirm the Qualifying Relationship — Biological Child
steps.description: For a biological child, the petitioner must be the legal (natural) parent. A child born in wedlock requires proof of the parent-child blood relationship and that the parents were married at or before the child's birth.
actions: Locate the child's birth certificate showing both parents' names
Confirm petitioner is named as a parent on the birth certificate
If the child was born out of wedlock to the father: review uscis.gov requirements for legitimation and legal relationship
Gather any additional documentation if parentage is not reflected on the birth certificate (e.g., DNA evidence, court-ordered parentage)
Review INA § 101(b)(1)(A) and (C) definitions at uscis.gov
steps.output: Biological parent-child relationship documented with birth certificate

steps.name: Confirm the Qualifying Relationship — Stepchild
steps.description: A stepchild qualifies as a 'child' for immigration purposes if the marriage creating the step-relationship occurred before the child's 18th birthday. The petitioner (the stepparent) must be the U.S. citizen who is married to the child's biological parent. See INA § 101(b)(1)(B).
steps.info: Important: The qualifying marriage must have taken place BEFORE the child turned 18. If the marriage occurred after the child's 18th birthday, the stepchild does not qualify as an IR-2 under this rule.
actions: Locate the marriage certificate showing the marriage between the U.S. citizen and the child's biological parent
Confirm the marriage occurred before the child's 18th birthday
Gather any divorce decrees or death certificates if either parent had a prior marriage
Locate the child's birth certificate identifying the biological parent
Confirm the marriage is currently valid and legally recognized
Review INA § 101(b)(1)(B) at uscis.gov for the stepchild definition
steps.output: Qualifying step-relationship confirmed: marriage before child's 18th birthday documented

steps.name: Confirm the Qualifying Relationship — Adopted Child
steps.description: An adopted child qualifies if the adoption was legally completed before the child turned 16 (or before age 18 if the child is an orphan or adopted with a sibling), and the child has been in the legal custody of and resided with the adopting parent(s) for at least 2 years. See INA § 101(b)(1)(E).
steps.info: Adoption cases can be complex. Requirements vary based on when the adoption was finalized, which country the child is from (Hague Convention vs. non-Hague), and whether the '2-year custody and residence' requirement is met. Consulting an immigration attorney is strongly recommended for adoption-based petitions.
actions: Locate the final adoption decree (court order) finalized before the child turned 16
Confirm the child has been in legal custody and physical residence with the petitioner for at least 2 years
Gather evidence of 2 years of joint residence (school records, medical records, utility bills naming both parties, etc.)
Confirm the adoption is recognized in both the country where it occurred and the United States
Review INA § 101(b)(1)(E) and the relevant USCIS adoption guidance at uscis.gov/adoption
For international adoptions: determine if the Hague Convention process or the non-Hague orphan process applies
Consult an immigration attorney given the complexity of adoption-based petitions
steps.output: Qualifying adoption relationship confirmed: adoption finalized before age 16 and 2-year custody/residence requirement documented

steps.name: Gather Supporting Civil Documents
steps.description: Before filing Form I-130, collect all civil documents needed to prove the qualifying relationship and the petitioner's citizenship. Documents not in English must be accompanied by a certified English translation.
actions: Petitioner's proof of U.S. citizenship (passport, birth certificate, or naturalization certificate)
Child's birth certificate (certified copy) — for biological or stepchild cases
For stepchild: marriage certificate of petitioner and child's biological parent; divorce decrees / death certificates for any prior marriages
For adopted child: final adoption decree; evidence of 2 years of legal custody and joint residence
Certified English translations for all documents not in English
Review the I-130 instructions at uscis.gov for the current required document list
steps.output: All required civil documents assembled and translated as needed

steps.name: Complete Form I-130
steps.description: Form I-130 (Petition for Alien Relative) is the USCIS petition establishing the qualifying relationship. Use only the current form version from uscis.gov.
actions: Download the current Form I-130 and instructions from uscis.gov
Complete Part 1 (petitioner information) and Part 3 (beneficiary/child information)
Answer all questions about prior petitions and, if applicable, prior marriages accurately
Sign and date the petition
Do not use expired or outdated form versions — always download from uscis.gov
steps.output: Form I-130 completed accurately and ready for filing

steps.name: Pay the I-130 Filing Fee
steps.description: USCIS charges a filing fee for Form I-130. The current fee is listed on the USCIS fee schedule. Fees are subject to change; always verify the current amount at uscis.gov before submitting.
actions: Check the current I-130 filing fee at uscis.gov/forms/filing-fees
Prepare acceptable payment (check, money order, or online payment per current USCIS instructions)
If filing online via myUSCIS: follow the online payment process
Retain your proof of payment
steps.output: Filing fee prepared or paid

steps.name: Submit Form I-130 to USCIS
steps.description: File Form I-130 online through myUSCIS or by paper mail to the appropriate USCIS lockbox or service center as specified in current form instructions.
actions: Determine the correct filing method (online at my.uscis.gov or paper mail) per current I-130 instructions on uscis.gov
Assemble the complete petition package: I-130, supporting documents, fee payment
If mailing paper: send via trackable/certified mail and keep the tracking number
If filing online: submit through your myUSCIS account
Keep a complete copy of everything submitted
steps.output: I-130 petition package submitted to USCIS

steps.name: Receive Form I-797C Receipt Notice
steps.description: After USCIS accepts the petition, it issues a Form I-797C Notice of Action confirming receipt. This notice includes the USCIS receipt number needed to track your case.
actions: Watch for the I-797C receipt notice by mail (allow several weeks)
Verify the receipt number, petitioner name, and child's name are correct
Save the receipt number — use it to track case status at egov.uscis.gov
Contact USCIS if the notice does not arrive within the expected timeframe per their website
steps.output: I-797C receipt notice received with correct case information

steps.name: Respond to Any USCIS Requests (RFE / NOID)
steps.description: USCIS may issue a Request for Evidence (RFE) or Notice of Intent to Deny (NOID) if additional information or documents are needed. Respond fully before the stated deadline.
actions: Monitor case status regularly at egov.uscis.gov or via the USCIS mobile app
If an RFE or NOID is received: read carefully, note the response deadline, gather all requested evidence
Draft a cover letter addressing each point raised
Submit the response before the stated deadline via the method specified in the notice
Keep a complete copy of the response and proof of submission
steps.output: All USCIS requests responded to timely and completely (or no RFE issued)

steps.name: Receive I-130 Approval (Form I-797)
steps.description: When USCIS approves the petition, it issues a Form I-797 approval notice and forwards the approved petition to the National Visa Center (NVC) for consular processing.
actions: Receive the I-797 approval notice and verify all names and dates are correct
Note that USCIS automatically forwards the approved I-130 to NVC for consular processing
Save the approval notice with your immigration records
Wait for the NVC welcome notification with the case number and invoice ID
steps.output: I-130 approved; petition forwarded to NVC for consular processing

stages.name: Stage II: NVC / CEAC Processing
stages.description: Pay fees → Submit DS-260 → Upload documents → Documentarily Qualified

steps.name: Receive NVC Welcome Notice
steps.description: The National Visa Center (NVC) notifies the petitioner by email or mail once the approved I-130 is received. The notice includes the NVC case number and invoice ID needed to access CEAC.
actions: Watch for the NVC welcome notice by email (or mail) with the case number and invoice ID
Log in or register at the Consular Electronic Application Center (CEAC) at ceac.state.gov
Review the immigrant visa process at travel.state.gov under 'Apply for an Immigrant Visa'
Ensure petitioner's and child's contact information is current with NVC
steps.output: NVC case number and invoice ID received; CEAC account accessible

steps.name: Pay NVC Immigrant Visa Fee
steps.description: NVC requires an immigrant visa application fee for the child beneficiary before processing continues. Pay through CEAC. The current fee amount is listed at travel.state.gov.
actions: Log in to CEAC at ceac.state.gov and select 'Pay Fees'
Verify the current immigrant visa fee at travel.state.gov/fees
Pay the immigrant visa fee for the child online through CEAC
Save or print the fee receipt from CEAC
steps.output: Immigrant visa fee paid and confirmed in CEAC

steps.name: Pay the Affidavit of Support Fee (if applicable)
steps.description: An Affidavit of Support processing fee may be required in CEAC. Check the current NVC fee schedule at travel.state.gov for whether this fee applies.
actions: Check CEAC and travel.state.gov for current Affidavit of Support fee requirements
If applicable, pay the fee through CEAC and save the receipt
steps.output: All NVC fees paid and receipts saved

steps.name: Complete Form DS-260 (Online Immigrant Visa Application)
steps.description: Form DS-260 is the immigrant visa electronic application submitted by or on behalf of the child beneficiary in CEAC. For minor children, a parent or legal guardian typically assists with completion.
actions: Log in to CEAC at ceac.state.gov and select 'Apply for an Immigrant Visa'
Complete all sections of the DS-260 for the child beneficiary accurately and truthfully
For minor children: a parent or legal guardian completes the DS-260 on the child's behalf
Answer all questions about the child's travel history and any prior immigration history
Review and submit the DS-260; record the confirmation number
Note: the DS-260 cannot be edited after submission without contacting NVC
steps.output: DS-260 submitted in CEAC; confirmation number recorded

steps.name: Prepare and Upload Civil Documents
steps.description: NVC requires the child's civil supporting documents uploaded to CEAC. Documents must be certified copies with certified English translations if not in English.
actions: Review the NVC required document list at travel.state.gov for the IR-2 category
Upload child's birth certificate (certified copy)
Upload child's valid passport (copy of bio page)
Upload police certificate(s) — required if the child is old enough to have lived in any country for 6+ months since age 16
For stepchild: upload marriage certificate and any divorce decrees or death certificates
For adopted child: upload final adoption decree and supporting custody/residence evidence
Obtain certified English translations for all non-English documents
Scan documents clearly and upload to CEAC under the correct categories
steps.output: All required civil documents uploaded to CEAC

steps.name: Prepare and Submit Form I-864 (Affidavit of Support)
steps.description: The U.S. citizen petitioner must complete Form I-864 Affidavit of Support showing that household income meets the required threshold (at least 125% of the federal poverty guideline). Upload it to CEAC.
actions: Download the current Form I-864 and instructions from uscis.gov
Complete Form I-864 with accurate household income and size data
Gather financial evidence: most recent federal income tax transcript or return, W-2s or 1099s, recent pay stubs, employer letter if applicable
If petitioner's income is insufficient: identify a qualified joint sponsor to complete a separate Form I-864
Sign the I-864 as directed in the current instructions
Upload completed I-864 and all financial evidence to CEAC
Review current income requirements (125% of HHS poverty guidelines) on uscis.gov
steps.output: Form I-864 and supporting financial documents uploaded to CEAC

steps.name: NVC Review and Case Complete
steps.description: NVC reviews all submitted documents and fees. When everything is accepted, NVC declares the case documentarily complete and schedules an interview appointment at the appropriate U.S. embassy or consulate.
actions: Monitor CEAC for NVC document requests or checklist items
Respond promptly to any NVC requests for additional documents
Once NVC approves all documents, await confirmation that the case is documentarily complete
NVC will schedule the child's interview at the U.S. consulate in the child's country of residence
Ensure the child's and petitioner's contact information with NVC is current
steps.output: Case documentarily complete; interview appointment scheduled by NVC

stages.name: Stage III: Medical + Interview
stages.description: Complete medical exam → Attend consular interview → Visa decision

steps.name: Receive Interview Appointment Notice
steps.description: The U.S. embassy or consulate sends an appointment letter with the interview date, time, location, and specific instructions. For minor children, a parent or legal guardian must typically accompany the child to the interview.
actions: Check CEAC and email registered with NVC for the interview appointment notice
Read the appointment letter carefully — note date, time, location, and instructions
Review the specific U.S. embassy or consulate's immigrant visa page for local requirements
Confirm the child's passport is valid for the interview date and travel
Confirm a parent or legal guardian is available to accompany a minor child to the interview
steps.output: Interview appointment letter received and details confirmed

steps.name: Locate an Authorized Panel Physician
steps.description: The immigration medical examination must be performed by a USCIS/State Department-designated panel physician in the country where the consular interview will take place.
actions: Find authorized panel physicians at travel.state.gov ('Find a Panel Physician')
Contact the physician's office to schedule the medical exam before the visa interview
Confirm required documents, vaccinations, and fees with the physician
For minor children: confirm whether a parent or guardian must accompany the child to the exam
steps.output: Panel physician identified and medical exam appointment scheduled

steps.name: Gather Medical Exam Documents
steps.description: Bring required documents and records to the medical exam. Confirm the specific requirements with the panel physician's office in advance.
actions: Child's valid passport
Passport-sized photographs (quantity per physician's requirements)
Child's vaccination records / immunization history
Any relevant prior medical records (chronic conditions, mental health history)
Interview appointment letter (some physicians require it)
Payment for the exam fee (verify with physician)
Parent or guardian accompanying a minor should bring their own ID
steps.output: All medical exam documents prepared

steps.name: Complete the Medical Examination
steps.description: The panel physician performs a physical examination, reviews immunization history, and administers any required vaccinations. Results are sealed and delivered to the consulate or transmitted electronically.
actions: Attend the medical exam on time with all required documents
Complete physical examination and any required lab tests
Receive required vaccinations per CDC Vaccination Requirements for Immigrant Visas (published at cdc.gov/immigrantrefugeehealth)
Age-appropriate vaccination waivers may apply for very young children — the physician will advise
If physician provides a sealed envelope: do not open it — bring it to the visa interview
Obtain the vaccination record (DS-3025 or equivalent) from the physician
steps.output: Medical examination completed; sealed results or electronic submission confirmed

steps.name: Prepare Interview Documents
steps.description: Assemble all documents needed for the consular interview. Review the specific consulate's instructions for any locally required additions.
actions: Child's valid passport
DS-260 confirmation page(print from CEAC)
NVC fee payment receipts
All original civil documents (birth certificate, police certificates if applicable) and translations
Stepchild: marriage certificate + divorce decrees/death certificates
Adopted child: final adoption decree + custody/residence evidence
Form I-864 Affidavit of Support (original signed copy)
Petitioner's proof of U.S. citizenship (copy)
Sealed medical examination envelope (if provided; do not open)
Two passport-style photographs per consulate instructions
Interview appointment letter
steps.output: Complete interview document package assembled

steps.name: Attend the Visa Interview
steps.description: The child (and accompanying parent or guardian) appears at the U.S. embassy or consulate for the immigrant visa interview. For minor children, the consular officer may ask about the qualifying relationship. Answer all questions honestly.
actions: Arrive at the consulate at the time specified in the appointment letter
Bring all assembled documents
Parent or guardian accompanies minor child as required by the consulate
Answer the consular officer's questions honestly and consistently with the DS-260
If a 221(g) notice is issued requesting additional documents, follow the instructions provided
If approved, the consulate will advise on visa issuance and passport return
steps.output: Visa interview completed; consular officer issues a decision

steps.name: Respond to Administrative Processing (if required)
steps.description: Some cases require additional administrative processing after the interview. The consulate will issue a Form 221(g) specifying what is needed. This is normal and may add time.
actions: If a 221(g) notice is received: read carefully and note the documents or actions required
Submit any requested documents or information promptly through the method specified
Monitor case status at ceac.state.gov
Allow the stated processing time before following up with the consulate
Contact the consulate only through official channels
steps.output: Administrative processing completed and resolved (or not required)

stages.name: Stage IV: Visa & Travel
stages.description: Receive visa → Prepare for travel to United States

steps.name: Receive Immigrant Visa in Passport
steps.description: If the consular officer approves the IR-2 visa, the embassy affixes the immigrant visa stamp to the child's passport and returns the passport, typically within a few business days.
actions: Wait for the child's passport to be returned (mail or pickup as specified by the consulate)
Verify the visa stamp: check name, date of birth, visa category (IR-2), and visa expiration date
The visa expiration date is the last date the child may use the visa to seek U.S. admission — plan travel before this date
Report any errors to the consulate immediately
steps.output: IR-2 immigrant visa affixed to passport and verified as correct

steps.name: Receive the Immigrant Visa Packet
steps.description: The consulate provides a sealed immigrant visa packet that must be presented to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) upon arrival. Do not open this envelope.
actions: Receive the sealed immigrant visa packet from the consulate
Do NOT open the sealed packet under any circumstances
Store the packet safely with travel documents
Note: the USCIS Immigrant Fee (for Permanent Resident Card production) may need to be paid — check current instructions at uscis.gov
steps.output: Sealed immigrant visa packet in hand and ready for travel

steps.name: Pay the USCIS Immigrant Fee (if applicable)
steps.description: Most immigrant visa holders must pay the USCIS Immigrant Fee online before or after arriving in the U.S. This covers Permanent Resident Card production. Verify current requirements at uscis.gov.
actions: Check whether the USCIS Immigrant Fee applies at uscis.gov/forms/uscis-immigrant-fee
If required, pay the fee at uscis.gov using the receipt number from the immigrant visa packet
Save the payment receipt
Confirm the current fee amount at uscis.gov before paying — amounts may change
steps.output: USCIS Immigrant Fee paid (if applicable)

steps.name: Book Travel to the United States
steps.description: The child must enter the U.S. before the immigrant visa expiration date. Book travel to arrive within the visa validity window.
actions: Confirm the visa expiration date (printed on the visa stamp)
Book an international flight arriving before the visa expiration date
Confirm the child's passport is valid for the duration of the trip
Make arrangements for the parent or guardian to travel with a minor child as appropriate
The child may enter at any U.S. port of entry staffed by CBP
steps.output: Travel booked; arrival date confirmed within visa validity window

stages.name: Stage V: U.S. Arrival
stages.description: Enter U.S. → CBP inspection → Green card delivery → Maintain LPR status

steps.name: Clear U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
steps.description: Upon arriving at a U.S. port of entry, the child presents travel documents to CBP. The CBP officer reviews the sealed visa packet and admits the child as a lawful permanent resident (LPR).
actions: Present the child's valid passport with the IR-2 visa stamp
Present the sealed immigrant visa packet to the CBP officer (do not open it yourself)
Complete any required CBP arrival forms per current CBP procedures
Answer CBP officer's questions honestly
Retrieve the electronic I-94 record at cbp.gov/i94 after entry (most air and sea ports)
steps.output: Child admitted to the U.S. as a lawful permanent resident; passport stamped

steps.name: Obtain I-94 Arrival Record
steps.description: CBP automatically creates an electronic I-94 arrival record for most air and sea entries. This is the official record of the child's admission as a lawful permanent resident.
actions: Visit cbp.gov/i94 to retrieve the electronic I-94 within a few days of arrival
Verify the I-94 shows the 'IR' class of admission and the correct name and date of birth
Print or save the I-94 record
If there is an error on the I-94, contact CBP to correct it promptly
steps.output: I-94 record retrieved and confirmed as showing IR lawful permanent resident admission

steps.name: Await the Permanent Resident Card (Green Card)
steps.description: USCIS will mail the Permanent Resident Card (Form I-551) to the U.S. address on file. The passport stamp and I-94 serve as temporary evidence of LPR status while the card is produced.
actions: Confirm USCIS has the correct U.S. mailing address
Check card production status at egov.uscis.gov if the card does not arrive within the expected timeframe
If the card is lost or never received: file Form I-90 (Application to Replace Permanent Resident Card)
The passport stamp may be used as temporary LPR evidence — verify current USCIS guidance on valid timeframe
steps.output: Permanent Resident Card (Green Card) received

steps.name: Apply for Social Security Number
steps.description: Most new LPRs are eligible for a Social Security Number. The application may have been initiated through the DS-260, or the child can apply at a Social Security Administration (SSA) office.
actions: If SSN was requested on the DS-260, wait for the SSN card to arrive by mail (allow several weeks after entry)
If not yet applied, locate the nearest SSA office at ssa.gov
Bring the Green Card (or passport stamp + I-94), passport, and a second form of ID
For a minor child: a parent or guardian applies on behalf of the child
Allow several weeks for the SSN card to arrive after application
steps.output: Social Security Number obtained or application submitted

steps.name: Enroll the Child in School (if applicable)
steps.description: As a lawful permanent resident, the child has the right to enroll in U.S. public schools. Contact the local school district for enrollment procedures and required documents.
actions: Identify the local public school district
Contact the school district enrollment office and inquire about required documents
Bring the child's LPR documentation, vaccination records, and prior school records
Complete any state-required enrollment forms
steps.output: Child enrolled in school or enrollment process initiated

steps.name: Understand LPR Rights and Responsibilities
steps.description: As a lawful permanent resident, the child (and the petitioner on their behalf) should be aware of ongoing obligations to protect LPR status, including maintaining U.S. residence and renewing the Green Card.
actions: Review USCIS guidance on LPR rights and responsibilities at uscis.gov/green-card
Note that extended absences from the U.S. (generally over 6 months or 1 year) can jeopardize LPR status — consult an immigration attorney if extended travel is planned
Renew the Green Card before it expires (Form I-90; most Green Cards are valid for 10 years)
U.S. federal income tax obligations may apply — consult a tax professional
steps.output: LPR rights and responsibilities understood; reminder set for Green Card renewal

steps.name: Consider Citizenship / Automatic Acquisition
steps.description: A child who is an LPR and under 18, residing in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of a U.S. citizen parent, may automatically acquire U.S. citizenship under the Child Citizenship Act of 2000 (INA § 320). Verify eligibility at uscis.gov.
steps.info: If the child automatically acquires citizenship, you may apply for a U.S. passport or a Certificate of Citizenship (Form N-600). Consult uscis.gov/n-600 for eligibility and application guidance.
actions: Review automatic citizenship criteria at uscis.gov/citizenship/citizenship-through-parents
If the child is under 18, is an LPR, and resides in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of a U.S. citizen parent: the child may have automatically acquired citizenship upon gaining LPR status
Apply for a Certificate of Citizenship (Form N-600) if desired, or apply for a U.S. passport
If the child does not automatically qualify, note the future naturalization eligibility period (generally 5 years as LPR, or 3 if residing with U.S. citizen parent)
Consult an immigration attorney if there is any question about automatic acquisition
steps.output: Citizenship acquisition evaluated; Certificate of Citizenship or future naturalization path identified

documents: Petitioner's Proof of U.S. Citizenship (Passport, Birth Certificate, or Naturalization Certificate)
Final Adoption Decree (for adopted child cases)
Evidence of 2-Year Legal Custody and Joint Residence (for adopted child)
Police Certificates (if child lived in any country for 6+ months since age 16)
Form I-864 Affidavit of Support (Signed Original)
